text: Hi, welcome to Dizima Architecture. My name is Andrea and I'm here with Michael from General
Electric. Hi Michael, welcome to the show. Hi, thank you. So what does your team do? So I work
on the cloud team at GE and we oversee all of the cloud accounts in the company. Wonderful.
So we're going to talk about virtual multi-factor authentication. For those viewers who don't know
what that is, describe it. Kind of the past couple years there's been a big kind of precedent it seems
that all every you know your Facebook, your Instagram, all that needs to have this 2FA so you
don't get hacked. So the same thing goes with your Amazon account. We want to make sure, especially
for the root account which has full access to everything, that you know that's locked down
and secure. So we ensure that we have virtual MFA on all of our root accounts also as part of kind
of keeping compliant with NGE. Wonderful. This is very interesting architecture and it runs on
AWS. So let's dive straight to it. I'm a user. I need access to an account. What do I do?
Okay, yeah. So you're going to go into API Gateway. You're going to have an API key
and you're going to put in the account ID you want to get access to and you're going to put in a reason
why you want to get access. So that's going to get sent from API Gateway which is going to go trigger
a Lambda. Okay. And that Lambda is going to go validate the request, make sure everything looks fine,
and then that's going to go kick off a stub function workflow. And then this is our approval workflow.
So that's going to go through. It's going to log the request down to Dynamo. So I'll request information.
And it's also going to kick off an email via SES to the approvers. Okay. When you said validate,
so Lambda validates if it's correct, how does it do the validation? Yeah. So part of the security of this is
that each request is tied to the person who submitted it. So at the end of this, it's going to generate a
request ID and that's going to get returned back to the user. That request ID is tied to your API key.
So if you give that request ID to your co-worker, it won't work. Let's dive straight into step functions.
Yeah. What is the process that is triggered? And is it purely Lambda based? Yeah. So the Lambda is going to go
kick off the stub function workflow, and that's going to go send out the email. And then there's,
we have some timeouts set in there as well. So you have, let's say, 24 hours to approve,
after which the request is going to get canceled. And then after approval, the user has, let's say,
another 24 hours to request the first readout. Those readouts, as soon as you request the first one,
you have 15 minutes to read out as many or up to six tokens. And what are you storing DynamoDB?
What specifically do you track? Basically all information about the request, like your IP,
your user agent, who, like the name of the person, the email of the person. And then we're going to
track who approved it, all that just for, so we can go back and track, you know, if anything was approved,
we can go back and see who approved it, why, when, all that information. And we can give that,
if we need to, to our security team. Wonderful. The approver approves it. Where is that token generated?
Prior to all this, all the account was added to the system. And all the secret hashes are stored
down here in Secrets Manager. So if I want to go, let's say, do a readout of one of these, I'm going
to go put in my account ID, and, or I'm going to put in my request ID, and then that's going to go
over to Lambda here. Okay. Which is going to, again, look at Dynamo and say, is this approved?
If so, you know, that comes back, gives, gives the information about the request. And then this
is going to go down into Secrets Manager. It's going to fetch and decrypt that secret. And then it's
going to generate the, the one-time six-digit code, and it's going to return that back to the user.
Okay. Very clever. How do you ensure security? How do you monitor, track?
Yeah. So like I said before, we have basically a full audit table of every request. In addition,
every time a request is, someone does a readout that generates a CloudWatch event, which is then sent
via an event bridge over to our security team.
This is a serverless architecture, and I see many interesting trends here. Tell me about the value or,
you know, the benefits that you achieved through deploying it through serverless.
Yeah. So prior to all this, and you know, prior to having to work remotely, we were storing all these
on those little hardware MFAs, which as you get more and more, and as time goes on, they tend to get out
of sync. So you have to go in, one, you have to like store them in a safe. And you know, if you have
binders and binders of that, that just starts to add up, you'd have a process to go get those physically.
And it was just becoming untenable to do that, especially at scale. So we converted all those
over to a virtual process. So now, you know, that we're working remotely, we can go from anywhere
in the world and request a token to get into an account. That's wonderful. Thank you so much for
walking us through this architecture. This is a highly secure virtual multi-factor authentication
platform that you built on AWS. Very unique. And thank you again for being on the show.
Thank you. And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
Thank you.
